elements.sent-2411-021: Planets form in the disks surrounding newly formed stars.
elements.sent-2411-022: Their atmospheric composition, as well as the composition of comets, is determined by the chemical makeup of these planet-forming disks, which itself is the result of the combination of ongoing chemical processes and the inheritance of molecular species from the earlier star-formation stages (e.g. \citealt{OB21, ObergEA23 ).}
elements.sent-2411-023: The Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) allows us to characterise the chemical inventory of planet-forming disks with unprecedented sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-024: The sensitivity enables us to detect multiple transitions of the same species that span a range of excitation conditions, even those with weaker line strengths.
elements.sent-2411-025: Detecting multiple transitions allows one, under the assumption of local thermal equilibrium (LTE), to directly infer both the gas thermal and excitation properties through a rotational diagram analysis (e.g. \citealt{GL99 ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: The use of multiple lines of the same molecule in order to constrain the physical structure of disks has a long history (see, e.g., \citealt{vZadelhoffEA01,DartoisEA03,LeemkerEA22 ).}
elements.sent-2411-027: Such studies have also highlighted the potential importance of non-LTE excitation in the upper layers of the disks (e.g., \citealt{vZadelhoffEA03,ThiEA04,WoitkeEA09 ).}
